name: azure-compute
description: Recommend Azure VM sizes, VM Scale Sets (VMSS), and configurations based on workload requirements, performance needs, and budget constraints. No Azure account required — uses public documentation and the Azure Retail Prices API. USE FOR: recommend VM size, which VM should I use, choose Azure VM, VM for web/database/ML/batch/HPC, GPU VM, compare VM sizes, cheapest VM, best VM for workload, VM pricing, cost estimate, burstable/compute/memory/storage optimized VM, confidential computing, VM trade-offs, VM families, VMSS, scale set recommendation, autoscale VMs, load balanced VMs, VMSS vs VM, scale out, horizontal scaling, flexible orchestration. DO NOT USE FOR: deploying VMs or VMSS, deploying apps (use azure-deploy), looking up existing VMs (use azure-resource-lookup), cost optimization of running VMs (use azure-cost-optimization), non-VM services like App Service or AKS.
capabilities: Recommend appropriate Azure VM size based on workload type, vCPU, RAM, and budget requirements
Compare VM families (General Purpose, Compute Optimized, Memory Optimized, GPU, HPC, Confidential)
Provide VM Scale Set vs single VM guidance with autoscaling recommendations
Query real-time Azure pricing from the public Retail Prices API
Explain trade-offs between VM options (cost vs performance, burstable vs dedicated)
Guide on Azure VM naming conventions and series suffixes
author: microsoft
securitySummary: All static findings are false positives. The detected patterns are code examples within markdown documentation showing API usage (curl, bash, PowerShell) and public Azure endpoints. The skill is a legitimate Microsoft Azure VM recommendation tool that uses only public, unauthenticated APIs. No executable code, no credentials, no deployments - purely informational recommendations.
valueStatement: This skill recommends Azure virtual machine sizes and VM Scale Set configurations based on workload requirements, performance needs, and budget constraints. It queries the public Azure Retail Prices API to provide cost estimates without requiring an Azure subscription.
limitations: Does not deploy VMs or VM Scale Sets - only provides recommendations
Does not have access to user's existing Azure resources or subscriptions
Cannot perform cost optimization analysis of running VMs
Does not support non-VM Azure services like App Service, AKS, or Azure Functions

useCases.targetUser: Developers and DevOps engineers planning new Azure deployments
useCases.title: New Application Deployment Planning
useCases.description: A development team is planning to deploy a new web application to Azure and needs to select the right VM size. They specify their workload as a stateless API backend with moderate traffic.

useCases.targetUser: Cloud architects and IT budget managers
useCases.title: Cost Optimization Review
useCases.description: A cloud architect needs to compare pricing across different VM families and sizes for a database server that requires high memory. They want to understand reservation vs pay-as-you-go costs.

useCases.title: ML Workload Sizing
useCases.description: A data science team needs GPU-enabled VMs for model training. They want to compare NC-series vs ND-series options and understand the pricing differences for their budget proposal.

promptTemplates.title: Basic VM Recommendation
promptTemplates.prompt: What Azure VM size should I use for a web server with moderate traffic?

promptTemplates.title: Workload-Specific Sizing
promptTemplates.prompt: I need a VM for a memory-intensive database workload with 64GB RAM. Which Azure VM family and size do you recommend?
promptTemplates.scenario: Memory-optimized VM selection with specific requirements

promptTemplates.title: GPU VM Comparison
promptTemplates.prompt: Compare the available GPU VM sizes for deep learning training. Include pricing estimates for ND-series in East US region.
promptTemplates.scenario: GPU VM comparison with pricing lookup

promptTemplates.title: VMSS vs Single VM Decision
promptTemplates.prompt: Should I use a single VM or a VM Scale Set for a stateless API that needs to handle variable traffic with autoscaling?
promptTemplates.scenario: VM vs VMSS architecture decision with autoscaling requirements

bestPractices: Always verify recommendations against current Azure documentation since VM capabilities and pricing change frequently
Use the B-series for dev/test and burstable workloads to minimize costs, then scale up to D or E series for production
Query the Azure Retail Prices API for real-time pricing and compare across regions to optimize costs
antiPatterns: Using general-purpose VMs for memory-intensive databases (should use E-series or M-series)
Deploying single VMs for stateless workloads that need autoscaling (should use VMSS)
Choosing the latest generation without considering pricing - older generations like Dv4 may be cheaper

faq.question: Does this skill require an Azure subscription?
faq.answer: No. The skill uses the public Azure Retail Prices API which is unauthenticated. You can get VM recommendations and pricing without an Azure account.

faq.question: Can this skill deploy VMs in Azure?
faq.answer: No. This skill provides recommendations only. For deployment, use the azure-deploy skill or Azure CLI commands.

faq.question: How accurate are the pricing estimates?
faq.answer: Pricing comes from Azure's public Retail Prices API and is updated daily. Actual prices may vary slightly and do not include taxes or region-specific discounts.

faq.question: What is the difference between B-series and D-series VMs?
faq.answer: B-series are burstable - they have a low baseline CPU performance with credits for bursts. D-series provide consistent, dedicated performance. B-series are cheaper for dev/test; D-series for production.

faq.question: When should I use VM Scale Sets instead of a single VM?
faq.answer: Use VMSS when you need autoscaling, high availability across fault domains, or multiple identical instances. Use a single VM for dev/test, jumpboxes, or workloads that never scale.

faq.question: Can this skill help with GPU VM selection?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill recommends GPU families (NC for training, ND for large-scale AI, NV for visualization) based on your workload type and provides pricing comparisons.
